primary_diagnosis.name: Post-COVID ME/CFS — severe post-exertional malaise (PEM) crash

pathophysiology_summary: ME/CFS post-exertional malaise involves a delayed, disproportionate and prolonged worsening of fatigue, cognition and sensory tolerance after minor exertion, reflecting dysregulated energy metabolism, autonomic and immune function. The crash produces near-paralytic fatigue, global cognitive slowing/word-finding difficulty, unrefreshing sleep and photophobia/phonophobia, mimicking stroke, sepsis or metabolic encephalopathy. However, a negative partner-assisted FAST, global symmetric (not focal) slowing, afebrile status, normal breathing, rousability and a stereotyped prior pattern point to a severe PEM crash. Critically, an ED transfer exposes the patient to light, noise and exertion that can markedly worsen the crash, so — provided the stroke/sepsis screen is clear — the correct action is supportive radical rest, hydration and telehealth follow-up with vigilant safety-netting, not routine escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe neuro/cognitive change demands a FAST/stroke screen first.
Exertion-triggered stereotyped crash on a known background is most likely.
Overlaps, but the clear exertional trigger and physical crash point to PEM.
FAST-negative, global symmetric slowing, afebrile, rousable, stereotyped pattern — a severe PEM crash.
Low probability but retained — any new focal/FAST-positive feature mandates 000.
Clear exertional trigger and physiological crash.
Working telehealth diagnosis: severe PEM crash; support with radical rest/hydration and telehealth follow-up, escalating to 000 only for FAST-positive, febrile, or breathing/consciousness changes.

red_flags.description: Any FAST-positive feature (facial droop, unilateral weakness, slurred speech) or a new sudden focal deficit
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate acute stroke — time-critical, needs 000 and thrombolysis pathway.

red_flags.description: High fever, becoming unrousable, or abnormal/very shallow breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate sepsis/encephalopathy or respiratory compromise requiring emergency care.

red_flags.description: Inability to maintain any oral intake / carer unable to cope
red_flags.clinical_significance: Prolonged severe crash risks dehydration and carer exhaustion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively sending a severe PEM patient to ED 'to be safe' when the stroke/sepsis screen is clear — the sensory/exertional load can markedly worsen the crash.
Accepting 'just a crash' without performing a partner-assisted FAST and sepsis screen.
Presenting LDN/NAD+/CoQ10/diets as treatments for the acute crash.
Advising 'push through', graded exertion during a crash, or unnecessary trips that worsen PEM.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a PEM crash and against acute stroke; positive FAST would mandate 000.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal neurological examination and confirmation of no subtle focal deficit
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Complete a partner-assisted FAST and sepsis screen; if clear, support radical rest, hydration and telehealth follow-up while avoiding a harmful ED transfer; escalate to 000 for any FAST-positive, febrile, unrousable or breathing change.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either missing a stroke by skipping the screen, OR harming the patient by an unnecessary ED transfer that worsens the crash.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recognise PEM; avoid graded exercise as a cure; support pacing and rest, and adapt care to minimise exertion including avoiding unnecessary in-person attendance during severe episodes.
Any facial droop, arm weakness or speech disturbance warrants immediate emergency assessment for stroke.
